description:
You are an integration developer who has just finished running a small set of HTTP endpoint tests.  
    Two plain-text log files already exist in the workspace:

        1. /home/user/api_test/logs/requests.log
        2. /home/user/api_test/logs/responses.log

    Objective (simple 3-to-4 command workflow):

        A. Archive the whole /home/user/api_test/logs directory into a compressed
           GNU‐tar file named 2024-logs.tar.gz located in a new directory
           /home/user/api_archives.

        B. Unpack (extract) that archive into
           /home/user/api_archives/extracted so the original *.log files re-appear
           there.

        C. Produce an integrity-check file called
           /home/user/api_archives/extracted_check.log that lists the exact byte-sizes
           of the two log files created by the extraction step.

    VERY IMPORTANT – required format of extracted_check.log:

        • The file must contain exactly two lines, no header line, no blank lines.
        • Each line must be a CSV record:    <filename>,<filesize_in_bytes>
        • Use the bare filename (not the path) and the decimal size in bytes.
        • Keep the order exactly as shown below.
        • Do NOT add spaces before or after the comma.

        Line 1  →   requests.log,38
        Line 2  →   responses.log,26

    When you are done, the directory structure should look like this (paths are absolute):

        /home/user/api_archives/
            ├── 2024-logs.tar.gz
            ├── extracted/
            │     ├── requests.log
            │     └── responses.log
            └── extracted_check.log

    Notes & constraints:

        • Do not use sudo or root-only locations.
        • Make sure the extracted_check.log content matches the byte counts shown
          above verbatim – an automated test will read and compare the file.
        • Any relative-path components stored inside the .tar.gz archive are
          acceptable; the test only verifies that the two *.log files are present
          inside /home/user/api_archives/extracted/ and that the CSV file exists
          with the exact text specified.
        • Total number of shell commands required is small (roughly
          “mkdir –p”, “tar …”, “tar …”, and “printf/echo … > …”).

    Finish the task in the terminal. No manual file editing outside the shell.
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent starts:

        Directory: /home/user/api_test/logs
            ├── requests.log        (content below)
            └── responses.log       (content below)

        Content of /home/user/api_test/logs/requests.log
        ------------------------------------------------
        GET /users
        POST /users
        GET /users/123
        [EOF]

        Content of /home/user/api_test/logs/responses.log
        -------------------------------------------------
        200 OK
        201 Created
        200 OK
        [EOF]

    • Each line shown above is terminated by a single Unix newline (LF, 0x0A).
    • That makes the exact byte-lengths:
          requests.log  → 38 bytes
          responses.log → 26 bytes

    Expected FINAL state after successful execution:

        1. Directory /home/user/api_archives exists.
        2. File   /home/user/api_archives/2024-logs.tar.gz  exists
           – must be a valid gzip-compressed tar archive containing both log files.
        3. Directory /home/user/api_archives/extracted exists and contains
           byte-for-byte copies of the two original log files.
        4. File   /home/user/api_archives/extracted_check.log  exists with
           exactly this 2-line content (including final LF at the end of line 2):

               requests.log,38\n
               responses.log,26\n

    The automated test will:

        • Confirm that 2024-logs.tar.gz is present and non-empty.
        • Extract 2024-logs.tar.gz into a temp location and verify that both
          requests.log and responses.log are inside the archive.
        • Verify that /home/user/api_archives/extracted/ actually contains the
          two files and that their SHA-256 hashes match the originals.
        • Read /home/user/api_archives/extracted_check.log and assert that its
          exact byte sequence equals:

              72 65 71 75 65 73 74 73 2E 6C 6F 67 2C 33 38 0A
              72 65 73 70 6F 6E 73 65 73 2E 6C 6F 67 2C 32 36 0A

          (hex representation of the two CSV lines + LFs).

    No other files or directories need to be modified or created.
